title: Stripe Keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Stripe TEST secret key detected in source code. Secret keys (sk_test_*) provide full API access to your Stripe account in test mode, allowing complete control over payment processing, customer data, subscription management, and financial operations. While test keys don't process real payments, exposing them creates security risks: attackers can access test customer data and PII, examine your payment integration logic and workflows, identify security vulnerabilities in your implementation, manipulate test transactions to understand fraud detection, consume API rate limits preventing legitimate testing, gather intelligence about your business model and pricing, access test webhook endpoints and event data, and potentially pivot to production systems if environment separation is weak. TEST MODE REMEDIATION REQUIRED: 1) Rotate the exposed test secret key immediately via Stripe Dashboard (Developers → API keys → Roll test key), 2) Update all development environments and CI/CD pipelines with the new test key, 3) Review test data access logs for unauthorized API calls during exposure period, 4) Audit test mode transactions and customer records for suspicious activity, 5) Verify environment separation between test and production (separate accounts, different credentials, isolated infrastructure), 6) Ensure test keys cannot access production resources, 7) Store test secret keys in environment variables or secret management systems (never in code), 8) Use separate test keys per developer/environment for access control, 9) Implement API usage monitoring and alerting in test mode, 10) Rotate test keys regularly as part of security hygiene, 11) Never commit secret keys to version control, 12) Use .env files or secret stores for local development, 13) Implement least-privilege API access with restricted keys where possible, 14) Document when and where the key was exposed, 15) Review code repositories and logs for additional exposed credentials.

CRITICAL: Stripe LIVE secret key detected in source code! Live secret keys (sk_live_*) provide unrestricted API access to your production Stripe account, enabling complete control over real payment processing, customer financial data, subscription billing, and revenue operations. Exposing live secret keys creates SEVERE security and financial risks: attackers can process fraudulent charges and refunds draining your account, access complete customer payment information including credit card details and PII (names, emails, addresses, phone numbers), steal sensitive financial data for identity theft and fraud, manipulate subscription billing to grant free access or extract funds, create unauthorized payouts and transfers to attacker-controlled accounts, access detailed transaction history and business intelligence, modify customer records and payment methods, cancel legitimate subscriptions causing service disruptions and revenue loss, issue fraudulent refunds depleting your balance, create disputes and chargebacks impacting your merchant standing, manipulate webhook endpoints to bypass security controls, access Connect platform data if you use Stripe Connect, compromise connected merchant accounts, exhaust your API rate limits causing service outages, and potentially face regulatory penalties and compliance violations for data breaches. IMMEDIATE CRITICAL ACTIONS: 1) REVOKE the exposed live secret key IMMEDIATELY via Stripe Dashboard (Developers → API keys → Roll live key - THIS IS URGENT), 2) Update ALL production systems, services, and deployments with the new live secret key within minutes, 3) Enable Stripe Radar fraud detection if not already active, 4) Review ALL transaction logs, charges, refunds, payouts, and transfers from the time of exposure for unauthorized financial activity, 5) Audit customer data access logs for suspicious queries or data exfiltration attempts, 6) Check for unauthorized webhook endpoint modifications or additions, 7) Review subscription modifications, cancellations, and billing changes, 8) Verify payout account details haven't been changed to attacker accounts, 9) Check Connect platform accounts for unauthorized access or modifications, 10) Monitor for fraudulent charge attempts in real-time (set up alerts), 11) Review and potentially freeze suspicious payouts pending investigation, 12) Contact Stripe Support immediately to report the security incident and get assistance, 13) Notify your security team and initiate incident response procedures, 14) Store live secret keys ONLY in production-grade secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager) with strict access controls, 15) NEVER commit live keys to version control under any circumstances, 16) Implement separate keys per environment and service with principle of least privilege, 17) Use restricted API keys with limited permissions where full secret key access isn't required, 18) Enable and monitor Stripe webhook signature verification, 19) Implement IP allowlisting for API access where possible, 20) Set up real-time alerts for unusual API activity, transaction patterns, and financial operations, 21) Review and remove the exposed key from all code repositories, documentation, logs, and configuration files, 22) Scan all systems and backups for additional exposed credentials, 23) Document the full exposure timeline and scope for compliance reporting, 24) Consider notifying affected customers if data was accessed (check regulatory requirements), 25) Review and strengthen your secrets management policies and developer training.

Stripe TEST publishable key detected in source code. Publishable keys (pk_test_*) are designed for client-side use in test mode and allow limited API operations like creating payment tokens and checkout sessions. While less sensitive than secret keys, exposing test publishable keys creates risks: attackers can identify your Stripe integration and infrastructure, analyze your payment flows and checkout implementation, potentially exploit client-side vulnerabilities, consume API rate limits for token creation, gather intelligence about your test environment setup, and use reconnaissance for more targeted attacks. TEST MODE REMEDIATION: 1) Consider rotating the test publishable key via Stripe Dashboard for security hygiene, 2) Review test API usage logs for unusual token creation patterns, 3) Ensure proper environment separation between test and production, 4) Store publishable keys in environment variables rather than hardcoding, 5) Use separate publishable keys per environment/developer, 6) Implement client-side security controls (CSP, origin validation), 7) Monitor for unusual client-side API activity, 8) Document exposure for security records, 9) Never commit keys to public repositories.

Stripe LIVE publishable key detected in source code. Live publishable keys (pk_live_*) are used in production client-side code for creating payment tokens, checkout sessions, and Stripe Elements. While designed for public use, exposing them unnecessarily creates security concerns: attackers can identify your production Stripe account and integration, analyze your payment implementation for vulnerabilities, potentially abuse client-side API calls and rate limits, gather business intelligence about your payment flows, use your publishable key to fingerprint your infrastructure, and combine with other vulnerabilities for payment manipulation attacks. LIVE MODE RECOMMENDATIONS: 1) While publishable keys are less sensitive, avoid committing them to public repositories, 2) Use environment variables for configuration management, 3) Implement proper client-side security controls (Content Security Policy, origin validation, HTTPS enforcement), 4) Monitor Stripe Dashboard for unusual API usage patterns from your publishable key, 5) Implement fraud detection using Stripe Radar, 6) Ensure webhook signature verification for server-side payment confirmations, 7) Never rely solely on client-side validation for payment security, 8) Use Stripe Elements best practices for secure card collection, 9) Review code for proper error handling that doesn't leak sensitive information, 10) Consider rotating publishable key if concerned about exposure, 11) Document exposure for security awareness, 12) Audit client-side code for additional security issues.

Stripe TEST restricted key detected in source code. Restricted keys (rk_test_*) provide limited API access with specific permissions in test mode, offering better security through principle of least privilege. While restricted keys are more secure than full secret keys, exposing them still creates risks: attackers gain insight into your permission model and API usage patterns, can abuse the specific permissions granted to this key, may consume API rate limits for allowed operations, gather intelligence about your integration architecture, and potentially combine with other vulnerabilities. TEST MODE REMEDIATION: 1) Rotate the exposed restricted test key via Stripe Dashboard (Developers → API keys → Restricted keys), 2) Review the specific permissions granted to this key and minimize them if possible, 3) Audit test API logs for unauthorized usage of this key's permissions, 4) Store restricted keys in environment variables or secret management systems, 5) Use separate restricted keys per service/function with minimal required permissions, 6) Monitor API usage for this key for unusual patterns, 7) Document exposure and review security practices, 8) Never commit restricted keys to version control.

IMPORTANT: Stripe LIVE restricted key detected in source code. Live restricted keys (rk_live_*) provide scoped API access to your production Stripe account with specific permissions, implementing security best practices through least-privilege access control. However, exposing restricted keys still creates significant security risks: attackers can abuse all permissions granted to this key, access production customer and payment data within the key's scope, manipulate resources the key has write access to, consume API rate limits affecting production services, combine key permissions with other vulnerabilities for privilege escalation, gather intelligence about your system architecture and permission model, and potentially pivot to more privileged access. LIVE MODE REMEDIATION REQUIRED: 1) Revoke the exposed live restricted key immediately via Stripe Dashboard (Developers → API keys → Restricted keys → Revoke), 2) Create a new restricted key with the same or reduced permissions, 3) Update all production services using this key with the new restricted key, 4) Review the specific permissions granted to this key and minimize to only essential operations, 5) Audit production API logs and transaction records for unauthorized operations using this key during exposure period, 6) Check for suspicious activity within the scope of the key's permissions (customer access, payment operations, data queries), 7) Store restricted keys in production-grade secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault), 8) NEVER commit restricted keys to version control, 9) Use separate restricted keys per microservice/function with minimal required permissions, 10) Implement API usage monitoring and alerting for this key's operations, 11) Review and strengthen access control policies, 12) Consider splitting permissions across multiple keys for better isolation, 13) Enable Stripe webhook signature verification for event-driven operations, 14) Document exposure timeline and scope for security incident records, 15) Review code repositories and logs for additional exposed credentials.
